tonal.title: Light & Tonal Control
tonal.description: The foundation of any edit. Proper tonal balance ensures your subject is visible and the mood is set correctly.
steps: Exposure: Multiplies overall brightness. Use this to correct 'underexposed' (too dark) or 'overexposed' (too bright) shots.
Contrast: Increases the difference between light and dark. High contrast creates drama; low contrast creates a soft, hazy look.
Highlights & Shadows: Drag Highlights left to recover details in bright skies. Drag Shadows right to reveal details in dark areas without affecting the whole image.
Whites & Blacks: Sets the 'true white' and 'true black' points. This ensures your image has a full dynamic range without looking 'muddy'.

colour.title: Colour & Emotion
colour.description: Color temperature is a powerful storytelling tool. Warmer tones (yellows) feel inviting, while cooler tones (blues) feel clinical or somber.
steps: Temperature: Corrects 'color casts' caused by different light sources (e.g., indoor tungsten vs. outdoor shade).
Vibrance vs. Saturation: Use Vibrance to boost muted colors safely. Use Saturation sparingly, as it can easily make skin tones look orange.
Hue: Rotates the entire color spectrum. Best used for creative effects or correcting specific minor color shifts.

mask.title: Local Masking (Selective Editing)
mask.description: In photography, you rarely want the same edit applied to the whole photo. Masking allows you to 'guide' the viewer's eye.
steps: Brush Tool: Paint exactly where you want an effect. Great for brightening a subject's face or darkening a distracting background.
Linear Gradient: Simulates a 'Graduated Neutral Density' filter. Perfect for darkening a bright sky while leaving the foreground untouched.
Radial Gradient: Creates a focal point. Use it to create a custom spotlight on your subject.
Shortcuts: Press 'B' to toggle Mask Mode once a mask is created.

dimension.title: Composition & Framing
dimension.description: How you frame your subject determines how the viewer perceives the story.
steps: Crop (Key: C): Use the 'Rule of Thirds' by placing your subject on the grid lines to create a more balanced, professional composition.
Aspect Ratios: 1:1 is for social icons; 16:9 is cinematic; 4:5 is the standard for portrait-oriented social posts.
Rotate: Level your horizons. A tilted horizon is often a sign of an amateur shot unless used for a specific 'Dutch Angle' effect.

history.title: The Editing Workflow
history.description: Editing is an iterative process. Never be afraid to experiment.
steps: Undo/Redo (Ctrl+Z / Ctrl+Y): Quickly toggle between versions to see if your change actually improved the image.
Compare (Space): The most important tool. Hold Space to see the original. If the original looks better, you've over-edited!
Jump in Time: Click any entry in the list to instantly revert the entire project to that specific moment.
